name: Commit Message
description: write a clear conventional commit from a staged diff
when_to_use: when the user asks to commit changes or write a commit message
body:
1. run `git diff --staged` and read what actually changed.
2. pick the type (feat/fix/refactor/docs/chore/test) and a scope from the touched area.
3. write a <=50 char subject in imperative mood, no trailing period.
4. add a body only if the why isn't obvious — explain intent, not the diff.
5. commit; do not push unless asked.

name: Resolve Conflict
description: work through a merge or rebase conflict safely
when_to_use: when the user hits merge conflicts during merge, rebase, or cherry-pick
body:
1. run `git status` to list conflicted files and the operation in progress.
2. open each file, read both sides — keep intent from both, not just one branch.
3. delete the `<<<<<<< ======= >>>>>>>` markers, leave valid code.
4. build/test the merged result before continuing.
5. `git add` resolved files, then continue the op (`--continue`), never blind-`--theirs`.
6. if it's a mess, `git merge --abort` and rethink the approach.

name: Write Dockerfile
description: author a lean reproducible Dockerfile for a service
when_to_use: when the user wants to containerize an app or write a Dockerfile
body:
1. pick a pinned slim base image matching the runtime (e.g. node:20-slim, python:3.12-slim).
2. use a multi-stage build: deps/build stage, then copy only artifacts into a small runtime stage.
3. copy lockfile first and install deps before app code so layer cache survives code edits.
4. run as a non-root user, set WORKDIR, expose the port, define a HEALTHCHECK.
5. add a .dockerignore (node_modules, .git, secrets) so context stays small.
6. prefer exec-form CMD/ENTRYPOINT so signals reach the process.

name: Set Up CI
description: scaffold a CI pipeline that lints, tests, and builds on push
when_to_use: when the user wants continuous integration or a github actions workflow
body:
1. detect the stack and existing scripts (package.json, Makefile, pyproject).
2. create a workflow triggered on push + pull_request to the main branch.
3. add steps: checkout, setup-runtime with dependency cache, install, lint, test.
4. matrix only over versions that actually matter; fail fast off for full signal.
5. cache the package manager dir keyed on the lockfile hash.
6. keep secrets in repo/org secrets, never inline; gate deploy jobs behind CI passing.

name: Read Stacktrace
description: trace an exception back to the real root cause
when_to_use: when the user pastes a stack trace or error and wants it diagnosed
body:
1. read the bottom exception type + message first — that's the actual failure.
2. for chained traces, find the deepest `caused by` / `during handling`.
3. walk frames top-down to the first one in the user's own code, not the library.
4. open that file:line and inspect the variables/null/typing at fault.
5. reproduce minimally, then propose the fix at the root, not a swallow.

name: Incident Postmortem
description: write a blameless postmortem after an outage
when_to_use: when the user wants to document an incident or postmortem
body:
1. state impact up top: what broke, who/how many hit, duration, severity.
2. build a UTC timeline — detection, escalation, mitigation, resolution.
3. separate trigger, root cause, and contributing factors; stay blameless.
4. note what went well and what slowed recovery honestly.
5. list action items with owners + due dates, each tied to a real gap.
6. add detection/alerting follow-ups so it's caught faster next time.

name: Roll Back
description: revert a bad deploy or commit cleanly under pressure
when_to_use: when a deploy or commit broke production and the user needs to undo it
body:
1. confirm what's live and what the last-known-good version/sha was.
2. prefer redeploying the good artifact or `git revert` over force-pushing history.
3. if a shared branch, never reset --hard; use revert so the undo is itself a commit.
4. roll forward db migrations carefully — many aren't reversible, check before reverting.
5. verify health/metrics recover after rollback.
6. capture the bad sha for the postmortem before moving on.

name: Debug Container
description: diagnose why a container crashes, restarts, or won't start
when_to_use: when a docker or k8s container is crashlooping or exiting unexpectedly
body:
1. `docker ps -a` / `kubectl get pods` — read status and exit code (137=OOM, 1=app err).
2. pull logs (`docker logs`, `kubectl logs --previous`) for the crashed instance.
3. for OOM, check memory limits vs actual usage; bump or fix the leak.
4. for config issues, exec a shell in or override entrypoint with sh to inspect env/files.
5. check the CMD actually exists and is exec-form; verify mounts and ports.
6. fix root cause, rebuild, and watch it stay up before declaring done.

name: Branch Cleanup
description: prune stale local and remote branches without losing work
when_to_use: when the user wants to clean up old or merged git branches
body:
1. `git fetch --prune` to drop refs deleted on the remote.
2. `git branch --merged main` to find branches safely folded in.
3. eyeball the list — never delete current, main, or in-flight work.
4. delete merged locals with `git branch -d` (lowercase, refuses unmerged).
5. for confirmed-dead remotes, `git push origin --delete <branch>`.
6. leave anything ambiguous alone and flag it for the owner.

name: Tag Release
description: cut a versioned release with a tag and changelog
when_to_use: when the user wants to publish a release or bump a version
body:
1. decide the semver bump from the changes (breaking=major, feature=minor, fix=patch).
2. update the version in the manifest and the CHANGELOG with grouped notes.
3. commit that, then `git tag -a vX.Y.Z -m` an annotated tag, never lightweight.
4. push commit and tag (`git push --follow-tags`).
5. let CI build from the tag; attach artifacts to the release page.
6. announce the highlights, not the raw commit log.

name: Bisect Bug
description: binary-search git history to find the commit that broke something
when_to_use: when a regression appeared and the user doesn't know which commit caused it
body:
1. nail a reliable repro and a known-good old commit.
2. `git bisect start`, mark `bad` (now) and `good` <old-sha>.
3. at each checkout, run the repro and mark `good`/`bad` honestly.
4. automate with `git bisect run <test-script>` when the check is scriptable.
5. read the diff of the offending commit bisect names.
6. `git bisect reset` to restore HEAD, then fix the root cause.

name: Secrets Hygiene
description: remove a leaked secret and stop it recurring
when_to_use: when a credential, key, or token got committed or exposed
body:
1. rotate/revoke the secret first — assume it's already compromised.
2. remove it from history (git-filter-repo or BFG), not just the latest commit.
3. force-push the cleaned history and have collaborators re-clone.
4. move the value into env vars or a secrets manager, reference not inline.
5. add the path to .gitignore and a pre-commit secret scanner (gitleaks).
6. audit logs for any use during the exposure window.

name: Healthcheck Probe
description: design liveness/readiness checks that fail for the right reasons
when_to_use: when the user needs health endpoints or k8s liveness/readiness probes
body:
1. split readiness (can serve traffic now) from liveness (process is wedged).
2. readiness should check real deps — db, cache — so traffic waits for them.
3. keep liveness cheap and dependency-free so a slow db doesn't kill pods.
4. set timeouts/thresholds above normal p99 to avoid flapping restarts.
5. return proper status codes + a tiny body; no heavy work per probe.
6. test by killing a dep and confirming only readiness drops.

name: Tail Logs
description: find the signal in production logs during an active issue
when_to_use: when the user is investigating a live problem and needs to read logs
body:
1. fix the time window to around the incident, in UTC.
2. filter by request id / trace id / user to follow one failing path end to end.
3. grep error+warn first, then widen to surrounding context lines.
4. correlate across services on the shared trace id, not guesswork.
5. note the first anomalous line — earlier than the loud errors usually.
6. capture the smoking-gun lines for the ticket before logs rotate out.
